subject: otolaryngology
title: Head and neck carcinomas
preambleMarkdown:
# Head and neck carcinomas



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Head and neck carcinomas is a clinical condition or syndrome in otolaryngology.
- This article focuses on ear, nose, throat, airway, hearing, vestibular, sinus, and head-neck pathology.
- The practical clinical priority is otolaryngology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Head and neck carcinomas to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Head and neck carcinomas is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is otolaryngology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Head and neck carcinomas depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In otolaryngology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Head and neck carcinomas.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Head and neck carcinomas disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Head and neck carcinomas should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Head and neck carcinomas first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Head and neck carcinomas, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Head and neck carcinomas, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused otolaryngology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Head and neck carcinomas presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Head and neck carcinomas with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Head and neck carcinomas is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Head and neck carcinomas should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Head and neck carcinomas is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses otolaryngology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Head and neck carcinomas, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Head and neck carcinomas may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Head and neck carcinomas patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Head and neck carcinomas, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Head and neck carcinomas in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Head and neck carcinomas, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Head and neck carcinomas does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Head and neck carcinomas may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Head and neck carcinomas, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Head and neck carcinomas as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Head and neck carcinomas draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Neoplasms

_Source lines: 24140-24321._

```text
NEOPLASMS
Acoustic Neuroma
A. General principles
l . Also referred to as vestibular schwannoma
2. N erve sheath tumor of the eighth cranial nerve
3. Originate in the medial internal auditory canal or lateral cerebellopontine
angle
4. These tumors are benign in nature and slow-growing, but they may inter
fere with the anatomy in this region via compressive mass effect and cause
symptoms .

B. Clinical features
l . These patients often present with hearing loss, which can be progressive and is
unilateral in most cases.

2. A large subset of patients with these tumors also present with tinnitus and
disequilibrium.

## 3 . Patients may also present with facial numbness and numbness in the

distribution of the sensory component of the facial nerve . They may also
experience effects on the motor component of the facial nerve.

C. Diagnosis
l . MRI with gadolinium contrast along with clinical correlation of symptoms is
considered the gold standard in securing this diagnosis.

2. CT scan with contrast can be used in situations where MRI is not readily
accessible. It is not as sensitive in diagnosing these tumors.

3. A standard auditory evaluation using pure-tone audiometry, speech
discrimination score , and acoustic reflex should be used to evaluate
patients who present with unilateral hearing loss, unilateral tinnitus , or
disequilibrium.

D . Treatment
l . Treatment options include surgical resection, radiation therapy, or
observation.

## 2 . Due to the benign, slow-growing nature of these tumors, many patients can be

managed conservatively with observation and at least yearly follow-up imaging
and audiometry, particularly in the instance of a small tumor in an elderly or
otherwise poor surgical candidate.

3. If the growth rate is more than 2.5 mm per year or patient experiences
signiHcant worsening of symptoms , intervention via either surgery or radiation
therapy should be considered.

4. Radiation therapy approaches include conventional fractionated radiation
therapy, stereotactic radiotherapy, stereotactic radiosurgery, and proton
beam therapy.

5. Radiation therapy is sometimes elected in patients with relatively well
preserved hearing with the goal of preventing permanent hearing loss anor
facial nerve dysfunction.

O T O L A R Y N G O L O G Y e 329
II. Glomus Tumor
A. General principles
l . Also referred to as paragangliomas
2. The most common of these is a carotid body tumor (60% ) . There are also
two glomus tumors in the temporal bone: the glomus tympanicum, which
arises in the middle ear, and glomus jugulare, which arises in the jugular
foramen .

3 . Females are affected more commonly than males (6: 1 ) .

B . Clinical features
l . Patients with a paraganglioma in the temporal bone may present with pulsatile
tinnitus and hearing loss.

## 2 . Physical examination of the tympanic membrane often reveals a reddish-blue

mass behind the tympanic membrane, due to the highly vascular nature of the
tumor. This will often blanch with positive pressure applied via pneumatic
otoscopy (Brown sign) .

## 3 . Some paragangliomas secrete catecholamines and may be associated with

sympathetic symptoms such as tachycardia, palpitations, flushing, diarrhea,
headaches, or poorly controlled hypertension.

4. Extensive glomus tumors, particularly those that arise in the jugular foramen,
may be associated with multiple ipsilateral cranial nerve palsies.

C . Diagnosis
l . High-resolution CT of the skull base is typically the initial study used to
evaluate these tumors .

2. MRI of the skull base may be useful to further delineate a tumor's association
with surrounding soft-tissue structures.

## 3 . Magnetic resonance angiography can also be useful to evaluate for intraluminal

involvement of the carotid artery or occlusion of the jugular vein and/or
sigmoid sinus.

D . Treatment
l . Complete surgical removal of these tumors using a microsurgical technique is
recommended.

2. Alternatives to surgical intervention-particularly for recurrent or
residual tumors-include external beam radiation therapy or stereotactic
radiosurgery in order to halt tumor growth and stabilize neurologic
symptoms.

Ill. Juvenile Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma
A. General principles
l . Occur primarily in young males
2. Highly vascular tumors , which arise near the junction of the posterior nasal
cavity and lateral nasopharynx
B. Clinical features
l . Nasal obstruction
2. Recurrent epistaxis
3. Nasal drainage , serous otitis media , and resultant conductive hearing loss may
also be present.

C. Diagnosis
l . Endoscopic examination of the nasal cavities and nasopharynx
2. CT scan with contrast is the preferred initial imaging modality, in order to
evaluate the surrounding bony structure as well as the vascularity of the
tumor.

3. MRI is useful in complement with the CT scan to delineate any intraorbital or
intracranial extension of the mass.

D . Treatment
l . Surgical resection
2. Radiation and chemotherapy may be considered for the treatment of poor
surgical candidates, recurrent disease , or significant intracranial extension that
cannot be treated surgically.

IV. Recurrent Respiratory Papillomatosis
A. General principles
l . Most common benign laryngeal tumor in children, usually diagnosed between
2 and 3 years of age
2. Caused most commonly by human papillomavirus (HPV) strains 6 and l l
B . Clinical features
l . Hoarseness
2. Abnormal cry
3. Advanced disease may be associated with dyspnea, stridor, and respiratory
distress.

C. Diagnosis: micro laryngoscopy with biopsy of the lesion to establish a definitive
diagnosis
D. Treatment
l . Microlaryngeal surgery with debulking of the lesions via C02 laser is the most
common treatment modality.

2. Patients often require multiple procedures up to the age of puberty, when the
disease tends to regress.

3. Tracheotomy should be avoided if possible due to the possibility of extralaryngeal
spread of the disease.
```

### Source Excerpt: Squamous Cell Carcinoma

_Source lines: 24322-24529._

```text
SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA
I. Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Oral Cavity
A. General principles
l . The oral cavity contains the lips, anterior two-thirds of the tongue , buccal mu
cosa, hard palate , floor of the mouth, alveolar ridges, and retromolar trigone.

## 2 . More predominant in males

3. Tobacco and alcohol use are synergistic in the development of squamous cell
carcinoma (SCC) .

4. Betel nut quid use is carcinogenic and is considered a risk factor for oral cavity
cancer in India and Southeast Asia.

B. Clinical features
l . Patients typically present with nonhealing ulcers or lesions in the oral cavity.

2. Patients may also present with unilateral otalgia, trismus, bleeding, or
dysphagia.

## 3 . Leukoplakia and erythroplakia are considered premalignant lesions and should

be monitored closely for malignant transformation.

C. Diagnosis
l . It is important to perform a thorough head and neck examination in any
patient with a suspicious lesion of the mouth.

2. CT anor MRI of the head and neck are appropriate to establish the extent of
the lesion as well as to evaluate for lymph node metastasis.

3 . Biopsy of the lesion is also needed to establish a histologic diagnosis.

4. Panendoscopy including direct laryngoscopy, esophagoscopy, and bronchoscopy
is generally performed to evaluate the extent of the lesion and to search for any
synchronous lesions.

D . Treatment
l . Primary resection of the tumor using a surgical technique is typically the first
choice.

2. Radiation therapy can be used postoperatively or in patients with tumors that
are difficult to manage surgically.

II. Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Oropharynx
A. General principles
l . The oropharynx consists of the posterior third of the tongue, tonsillar fossa,
soft palate, and the posterior pharyngeal wall.

2. The chances of occurrence increase with the consumption of alcohol and
tobacco .

O T O L A R Y N G O L O G Y e 33 1
B. Clinical features
l . Signs of symptoms include sore throat, dysphagia , odynophagia, and
bleeding.

2. These tumors are often silent and difficult to detect in the early stages of the
disease.

## 3 . Patients can also present with unilateral otalgia , which is referred pain from

the oropharynx.

C. Diagnosis
l . It is important to perform a thorough head and neck examination on any
patient with a suspicious lesion of the mouth.

2. Panendoscopy including direct laryngoscopy, esophagoscopy, and bronchos
copy is generally performed to evaluate the extent of the lesion and to search
for any synchronous lesions.

3. Biopsy of the lesion is also needed to establish a histologic diagnosis .

4. CT and/or MRI of the head and neck should be done to evaluate the extent of
tumor involvement and lymph node disease .

## 5 . Positron emission tomography (PET) scans may be ordered a s part o f

the workup for patients with advanced disease t o evaluate for metastatic
disease.

D . Treatment
l . Chemotherapy combined with radiation therapy is typically the treatment of
choice for sec of the oropharynx.

2. Surgical resection is generally reserved for patients who fail chemoradiation.

Ill. Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Nasopharynx
A. General principles
l . More common in Chinese Americans
2. Associated with Epstein-Barr virus, sawdust inhalation, and smoke inhalation
3. Smoking increases the risk of disease.

B. Clinical features
l . Cervical adenopathy or neck mass
2. Unilateral otitis media with euusion with associated hearing loss
3. Nasal obstruction

## 4. Epistaxis

C. Diagnosis
l . Nasopharyngoscopy
2. Biopsy of the lesion with endoscopic guidance
3. CT and/or MRI to evaluate for involvement of tumor
D. Treatment
l . Patients with nasopharyngeal carcinoma usually undergo radiation therapy.

2. This is the treatment of choice because it is difficult to obtain surgical margins
in the nasopharynx.

IV. Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Larynx
A. General principles
l . The vast majority of patients with laryngeal cancer have an extensive history
of alcohol and tobacco use.

2 . Gastroesophageal reflux disease is controversial risk factor.

3. HPV infection is a risk factor for all head and neck S C C , including the larynx.

4. Laryngeal cancers are divided into supraglottic , glottic, and subglottic.

B. Clinical features
l . Signs and symptoms include dysphonia, sore throat, dysphagia, ear pain,
dyspnea, stridor, and hemoptysis .

## 2 . Physical examination should include a complete head and neck exam ,

including a thorough neck examination, to evaluate for any lymph node
disease.

3. Flexible laryngoscopy is typically performed in clinic to evaluate the extent of
laryngeal involvement, vocal cord mobility, and airway patency.

C. Diagnosis
l . CT anor MRI of the head and neck should be done to evaluate the extent of
tumor involvement and lymph node disease.

## 2 . Panendoscopy including direct laryngoscopy is typically performed in the

operating room to evaluate the extent of tumor involvement for surgical
planning. Biopsies are also performed at this time for histologic diagnosis.

3. PET scans are typically ordered as part of the workup for patients with ad
vanced laryngeal cancer to evaluate for metastatic disease.

D . Treatment
l . Medical management: Radiation and chemotherapy are alternatives to surgery.

2. Surgical management
a. Tracheotomy may need to be performed prior to definite surgical treatment
for large lesions causing airway obstruction.

b . Microlaryngoscopy with endoscopic excision can be done for some small
T l lesions.

c. Partial laryngectomy
( l ) Involves removing the affected portions of the larynx while leaving
enough of the unaffected larynx to maintain adequate speech and
swallowing
(2) Options include hemilaryngectomy, supraglottic laryngectomy,
supracricoid laryngectomy, and near-total laryngectomy.

(3) Patient typically must have good preoperative lung function.

d. Total laryngectomy
( l ) Involves removing the entire larynx including the hyoid bone and
cricoid cartilage
(2) It is usually reserved for patients with extensive disease.

(3) Patients have a permanent tracheal stoma after this procedure .

Cardiothoracic surgery is a broad Held that includes acquired and congenital disease.

It also comprises treatment for some of the leading causes of death: coronary artery
disease (CAD) and lung cancer. This chapter discusses diagnostic testing, cardiac disease,
pulmonary disease, and mediastinal and chest wall diseases.
```
